nativeRange.habitat: Found over rubble patches on seaward and back-reef slopes, generally at depths between 7 and 55 metres.

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched frozen brine shrimp, and other meaty foods along with high-quality marine flake and pellet foods. In the wild it forages over reef rubble for small invertebrates and zooplankton.

avoidWith: other Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus species in small tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Jokuyken15, 5459x3639. Tack-sharp full-body aquarium male showing the diagnostic magenta-red body with yellow lateral stripe, blue-green facial markings and purple caudal fin; best specimen available.
Debra Baker, 1414x1137. In-situ wild individual off Ile Tenia, New Caledonia showing red/yellow lateral striping; slight blue-water cast, whole fish framed. In-situ context extra.
Debra Baker, 1141x961. Second in-situ New Caledonia individual, different angle showing paler duller phase with red/yellow stripes; blue-water cast. Variety extra.

summary: A vividly pink-red fairy wrasse from the Great Barrier Reef and New Caledonia; generally considered reef-safe, peaceful, and well suited to medium-to-large reef aquariums with a tight-fitting lid.
careNotes: Laboute's Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus laboutei) was described by Randall & Lubbock in 1982 and named for Pierre Laboute, who first photographed the species off New Caledonia. In the wild it is reported from New Caledonia, the Loyalty Islands, and the Great Barrier Reef, living at depths of roughly 7 to 55 metres over rubble patches on seaward and back-reef slopes. Like other members of the genus Cirrhilabrus, this species has been reported to jump, and a tight-fitting aquarium lid is strongly advised. The Laboute's Fairy Wrasse is generally considered peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates, though males tend to be aggressive toward other male fairy wrasses and toward similar Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus species, particularly in smaller aquariums. The standard approach is to keep one male per tank, either alone or with a small group of females; introducing females before or at the same time as the male helps reduce conflict. Cirrhilabrus species are protogynous sequential hermaphrodites — meaning individuals are born female and the dominant individual in a group can change sex to become male. This produces a harem-based social structure in the wild. Males display intense magenta-red coloration with yellow striping that brightens further during courtship; females are duller yellowish-brown with magenta stripes, and subadult females can be identified by a distinctive black spot on the caudal fin. The species is assessed as Least Concern by the IUCN (2009).
breedingNotes: In the wild, Cirrhilabrus laboutei has been recorded engaging in distinct pairing during breeding, consistent with the harem-based social structure typical of the genus. Captive spawning has not been reliably reported for this species. Replicating harem conditions — one male kept with several females — in a larger reef system may elicit nuptial display behaviour, though successful captive reproduction remains undocumented.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verified taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria/misc), maximum size (12.0 cm TL), native range (Western Central Pacific: New Caledonia and Loyalty Islands to the Great Barrier Reef), depth range (7-55 m), habitat (rubble patches on seaward and back-reef slopes), trophic level (3.4), distinct pairing during breeding, and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2009). Size disagreement with LiveAquaria (12 vs ~14 cm) is small and likely reflects TL-vs-maximum-recorded difference; adopted a 10-14 cm range that brackets both.
